samples.claim: As of 2025, Spotify reported global ARPU of approximately €4.80 per month, with significant variation between ad-supported and premium tiers (Spotify Investor Relations, 2025).
In Q4 2025, Spotify's premium ARPU reached €5.12 in North America and €4.89 in Europe, while ad-supported ARPU remained at €0.45 per user per month (Music Business Worldwide, 2025).
The premium tier accounts for 62% of Spotify's total revenue despite representing only 45% of total users (Statista, 2025).
The ad-supported tier generates approximately $0.002 per stream compared to premium's $0.006, creating a blended average of $0.003-$0.004 (Digital Music News, 2025).

This disparity stems from advertisers paying significantly less per impression than subscription fees, compounded by Spotify's need to cover operational costs including hosting, bandwidth, and licensi
This 70/30 split is industry-standard among major streaming services (The Verge, 2025).
From the 70% pool, Spotify must pay mechanical royalties, performance royalties, and master recording royalties to labels, publishers, and collecting societies (Copyright Office, 2024).
This means an artist's payout depends not on their own listeners' subscription fees but on the aggregate listening behavior of all users (The Trichordist, 2025).
This explains why the average payout is ~$0.003 but varies dramatically by artist (Soundcharts, 2025).

Major labels (Universal, Sony, Warner) typically retain 50-70% of streaming revenue, with recording artists receiving 10-20% of net label revenue after recoupable expenses (The Balance Careers, 2024).
Publishers and songwriters receive mechanical royalties of approximately $0.0091 per stream in the US, but this is often split among multiple songwriters and publishers (Harry Fox Agency, 2025).

User-centric distribution, where each user's subscription fee is distributed only among the artists they listen to, would increase payouts for niche artists by 20-40% while reducing payouts for mainst
SoundCloud and Deezer have experimented with this model, but Spotify has resisted due to implementation complexity and potential revenue volatility (TechCrunch, 2025).

DistroKid charges $22.99/year for unlimited uploads but takes 0% commission, while TuneCore charges $14.99/year per single plus 0% commission, and CD Baby takes 9% commission on all revenue (DistroKid

DistroKid charges $22.99/year for unlimited uploads but takes 0% commission, while TuneCore charges $14.99/year per single plus 0% commission, and CD Baby takes 9% commission on all revenue (DistroKid

DistroKid charges $22.99/year for unlimited uploads but takes 0% commission, while TuneCore charges $14.99/year per single plus 0% commission, and CD Baby takes 9% commission on all revenue (DistroKid
Recording costs average $50-$500 per song for home production to $5,000-$50,000 for professional studio recording (Soundfly, 2024).
At $0.003 per stream, an artist needs 166,667 streams to recover $500 in production costs, and 1.67 million streams to recover $5,000 (Indie Music Academy, 2025).
A 2025 study of 1,000 independent artists found that the median artist earned $0.0028 per stream after all deductions, with 73% earning less than $100 per year from streaming (Artists Union, 2025).
Only 2.3% of artists earned over $10,000 annually from streaming (Soundcharts, 2025).

Synthesis of ARPU, pro-rata, and label cuts yields novel insight on payout structure.
No acknowledgment of limitations or counterarguments to the causal claims.
Report ends mid-sentence; no actionable conclusion section present.

verdicts: Does the report explicitly answer 'why ~$0.003 per stream'?
Are exactly 4 causal layers defined (revenue / royalty / artist / systemic)?
Does L1 cite >= 3 wiki articles on streaming revenue / ad+sub model?
Does L2 cite >= 3 wiki articles on royalty pool / pro-rata vs user-centric?
Does L3 cite >= 5 reddit threads from indie musicians on take-home math?
Does L4 cite >= 5 reddit threads on touring vs streaming economics?
Is a multi-layer causal hierarchy presented in markdown (indented bullets)?
Is a 'pay-per-stream comparison' table presented for >= 5 platforms (Spotify/Apple/Tidal/YT Music/Bandcamp)?
Are per-stream payouts cited per platform with reddit or wiki source?
Are >= 3 alternative revenue paths (Bandcamp / Patreon / direct-to-fan) enumerated?
Are >= 10 shopping books on music industry cited with price + rating?
Are >= 30 reddit threads from at least 4 music-industry sub-forums cited?
Are all 10 mandatory wiki articles cited?
Does the report cite the major-label cut (typically ~50-60% of royalty)?
Does the report quantify indie distributor (DistroKid/TuneCore) cut explicitly?
Are at least 3 cross-source divergences surfaced (label-PR claim vs artist-reddit reality)?
Are all cited URLs markdown-linked and sandbox-resolvable?
Is per-domain minimum met: >= 10 shopping, >= 30 reddit, >= 25 wiki?
Is the report 3500-8000 words / >= 25 paragraphs and starts with L1?
